title: Cache Read Operation Algorithm
description: Demonstrates how cache read hit and miss are handled
code:
CACHE_READ(address):
 offset = address % cacheLineSize
 index = (address / cacheLineSize) % numSets
 tag = address / (cacheLineSize * numSets)
 
 FOR each way IN set[index]:
 IF way.valid AND way.tag == tag:
 // Cache Hit
 updateLRU(set[index], way)
 RETURN way.data[offset]
 
 // Cache Miss - Fetch from main memory
 block = MAIN_MEMORY_READ(address, cacheLineSize)
 
 // Find replacement victim
 victim = SELECT_REPLACEMENT(set[index])
 
 IF victim.dirty == TRUE:
 MAIN_MEMORY_WRITE(victim.tag, victim.data)
 
 // Load new block
 victim.tag = tag
 victim.data = block
 victim.dirty = FALSE
 victim.valid = TRUE
 
 RETURN victim.data[offset]
explanation: This algorithm shows the complete cache read operation. First, it computes offset, index, and tag from the address. Then it searches the appropriate set for a matching valid line. On a hit, data is returned and LRU is updated. On a miss, the block is fetched from main memory, a victim is selected for replacement, and the new block is loaded.

complexity.time: O(associativity) for search, O(cacheLineSize) for memory transfer

title: LRU Replacement Algorithm
description: Implements Least Recently Used replacement for set-associative cache
code:
// Initialize LRU counters for a set
INIT_LRU_SET(set):
 FOR each way IN set:
 way.lruCounter = way.wayNumber

// Update LRU after access
UPDATE_LRU(set, accessedWay):
 accessedCounter = accessedWay.lruCounter
 FOR each way IN set:
 IF way.lruCounter < accessedCounter:
 way.lruCounter = way.lruCounter + 1
 accessedWay.lruCounter = 0

// Select victim for replacement
SELECT_LRU_VICTIM(set):
 maxCounter = -1
 victim = NULL
 FOR each way IN set:
 IF way.lruCounter > maxCounter:
 maxCounter = way.lruCounter
 victim = way
 RETURN victim
explanation: The LRU algorithm maintains counters for each way in a set. After each access, the accessed way gets counter 0, and all counters for ways that were more recently used are incremented. The victim is the way with the highest counter (least recently used).

title: Write-Back Cache Write Operation
description: Demonstrates write-back policy with write-allocate on miss
code:
CACHE_WRITE(address, data):
 offset = address % cacheLineSize
 index = (address / cacheLineSize) % numSets
 tag = address / (cacheLineSize * numSets)
 
 FOR each way IN set[index]:
 IF way.valid AND way.tag == tag:
 // Write Hit
 way.data[offset] = data
 way.dirty = TRUE
 updateLRU(set[index], way)
 RETURN
 
 // Write Miss - Write Allocate
 // Find victim
 victim = SELECT_REPLACEMENT(set[index])
 
 // Write back if dirty
 IF victim.dirty == TRUE:
 victimAddress = victim.tag * cacheLineSize * numSets + index * cacheLineSize
 MAIN_MEMORY_WRITE(victimAddress, victim.data)
 
 // Fetch block from memory
 block = MAIN_MEMORY_READ(address, cacheLineSize)
 
 // Load new block and write
 victim.tag = tag
 victim.data = block
 victim.data[offset] = data
 victim.dirty = TRUE
 victim.valid = TRUE
explanation: This algorithm implements write-back with write-allocate. On a write hit, data is written to cache and dirty bit is set. On a write miss, the block is first loaded into cache (write-allocate), then the write is performed. The dirty bit indicates the block has been modified and needs to be written back on eviction.
